'''Takano Shrine''' (高野神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tsuyama City|ja|津山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), a disputed {{ill|Ichinomiya|ja|一宮}} of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}}. Under the modern shrine ranking system, it was designated as a [[Ken-sha]] (prefectural shrine).
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated in the western part of Tsuyama City, with the sacred Mount Kami to the northwest and the Sarayama hills of Kume to the south, overlooking the Yoshii River. It is one of the three major shrines of Mimasaka Province, alongside {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}} and {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}.<ref>The other two major shrines of Mimasaka are Nakayama Shrine and Mimasaka Sōja Shrine.</ref>
Since the medieval period, the shrine enjoyed reverence from local lords, including the daimyō of the {{ill|Tsuyama Domain|ja|津山藩}}, the Mori family. The current main shrine, reconstructed by the Mori clan during the Edo period, is a designated Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture. The shrine also houses national-level Important Cultural Properties, such as statues of Zuishin (guardian deities).
== Deities ==
; Primary deity * '''Hiko Nagisa Take Uga Fuki Aezunomikoto''' (彦波限建鵜葺草葺不合尊) – The main enshrined deity.
; Associated deities * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} – Deity of {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}. * {{ill|Kagami Tsukuri no Mikoto|ja|鏡作命}} – Deity of {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}}, the Ichinomiya of Mimasaka Province.
== History ==
=== Foundation === According to shrine tradition, worship began at the Onokoro Iwa (碫馭盧岩) on the Yoshii River, which was regarded as a sacred rock (iwakura). The shrine itself was established during the second year of Emperor Ankan’s reign (534 CE) and subsequently moved to its current location.<ref name="origin">[http://space.geocities.jp/ninomiya_takano/contents/rekishi/yuisho/yuisho.html Takano Shrine History] (official site).</ref>
=== Development === The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a Shikinaisha of Mimasaka Province. Some theories identify the shrine in question as Takano Shrine in Ninomiya, while others propose the {{ill|Takano Shrine (Takahon-gō)|ja|高野神社 (津山市高野本郷)}} in Takahon-gō. Both remain disputed.
The shrine gained prominence in the Kamakura period, receiving patronage from notable figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, {{ill|Yamana Tokiharu|ja|山名時春}}, {{ill|Mōri Motonari|ja|毛利元就}}, and {{ill|Kobayakawa Takakage|ja|小早川隆景}}. In the Edo period, the Mori family, daimyō of the Tsuyama Domain, rebuilt the shrine. Later, the Matsudaira clan, successors to the domain, also supported the shrine.
During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was classified as a [[Ken-sha]] under the modern shrine ranking system.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Nationally Designated Important Cultural Properties === * '''Wooden Inscription Plaque''' – Designated August 2, 1901. * '''Wooden Zuishin Statues (2 figures)''' – Crafted in 1162. Designated June 28, 1956. * '''Wooden Lion Statues (2 pairs)''' – Dated to the 10th and 12th centuries. Considered among the oldest examples of this type in Japan. Designated June 8, 2004.<ref>“Newly Designated Cultural Properties,” *Monthly Cultural Properties*, Issue 489 (2004), pp. 24–25.</ref>
=== Prefectural and Municipal Designations === * '''Main Shrine''' – Prefectural designation in 1983. * '''Zuishin Gate and Roof Covering''' – Municipal designation in 1999. * '''Mukuno Tree in Unate Forest''' – Municipal designation in 1973.
== Festivals ==
* '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Summer Festival''' (July 18) * '''Autumn Festival''' (Sunday closest to October 22)
== Access == ; Address * 601 Ninomiya, Tsuyama City, Okayama Prefecture
; Transportation * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Innosho Station|ja|院庄駅}} on the {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Kishin Line|ja|姫新線}}.
== Surrounding Area == * '''Miwayama Burial Mounds''' – The largest keyhole-shaped burial mounds in Mimasaka. * '''Sakaku Shrine'''

'''Takano Shrine''' (高野神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tsuyama City|ja|津山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), a disputed {{ill|Ichinomiya|ja|一宮}} of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}}. Under the modern shrine ranking system, it was designated as a [[Ken-sha]] (prefectural shrine).
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated in the western part of Tsuyama City, with the sacred Mount Kami to the northwest and the Sarayama hills of Kume to the south, overlooking the Yoshii River. It is one of the three major shrines of Mimasaka Province, alongside {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}} and {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}.<ref>The other two major shrines of Mimasaka are Nakayama Shrine and Mimasaka Sōja Shrine.</ref>
Since the medieval period, the shrine enjoyed reverence from local lords, including the daimyō of the {{ill|Tsuyama Domain|ja|津山藩}}, the Mori family. The current main shrine, reconstructed by the Mori clan during the Edo period, is a designated Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture. The shrine also houses national-level Important Cultural Properties, such as statues of Zuishin (guardian deities).
== Deities ==
; Primary deity * '''Hiko Nagisa Take Uga Fuki Aezunomikoto''' (彦波限建鵜葺草葺不合尊) – The main enshrined deity.
; Associated deities * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} – Deity of {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}. * {{ill|Kagami Tsukuri no Mikoto|ja|鏡作命}} – Deity of {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}}, the Ichinomiya of Mimasaka Province.
== History ==
=== Foundation === According to shrine tradition, worship began at the Onokoro Iwa (碫馭盧岩) on the Yoshii River, which was regarded as a sacred rock (iwakura). The shrine itself was established during the second year of Emperor Ankan’s reign (534 CE) and subsequently moved to its current location.<ref name="origin">[http://space.geocities.jp/ninomiya_takano/contents/rekishi/yuisho/yuisho.html Takano Shrine History] (official site).</ref>
=== Development === The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a Shikinaisha of Mimasaka Province. Some theories identify the shrine in question as Takano Shrine in Ninomiya, while others propose the {{ill|Takano Shrine (Takahon-gō)|ja|高野神社 (津山市高野本郷)}} in Takahon-gō. Both remain disputed.
The shrine gained prominence in the Kamakura period, receiving patronage from notable figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, {{ill|Yamana Tokiharu|ja|山名時春}}, {{ill|Mōri Motonari|ja|毛利元就}}, and {{ill|Kobayakawa Takakage|ja|小早川隆景}}. In the Edo period, the Mori family, daimyō of the Tsuyama Domain, rebuilt the shrine. Later, the Matsudaira clan, successors to the domain, also supported the shrine.
During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was classified as a [[Ken-sha]] under the modern shrine ranking system.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Nationally Designated Important Cultural Properties === * '''Wooden Inscription Plaque''' – Designated August 2, 1901. * '''Wooden Zuishin Statues (2 figures)''' – Crafted in 1162. Designated June 28, 1956. * '''Wooden Lion Statues (2 pairs)''' – Dated to the 10th and 12th centuries. Considered among the oldest examples of this type in Japan. Designated June 8, 2004.<ref>“Newly Designated Cultural Properties,” *Monthly Cultural Properties*, Issue 489 (2004), pp. 24–25.</ref>
=== Prefectural and Municipal Designations === * '''Main Shrine''' – Prefectural designation in 1983. * '''Zuishin Gate and Roof Covering''' – Municipal designation in 1999. * '''Mukuno Tree in Unate Forest''' – Municipal designation in 1973.
== Festivals ==
* '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Summer Festival''' (July 18) * '''Autumn Festival''' (Sunday closest to October 22)
== Access == ; Address * 601 Ninomiya, Tsuyama City, Okayama Prefecture
; Transportation * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Innosho Station|ja|院庄駅}} on the {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Kishin Line|ja|姫新線}}.
== Surrounding Area == * '''Miwayama Burial Mounds''' – The largest keyhole-shaped burial mounds in Mimasaka. * '''Sakaku Shrine'''

'''Takano Shrine''' (高野神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tsuyama City|ja|津山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), a disputed {{ill|Ichinomiya|ja|一宮}} of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}}. Under the modern shrine ranking system, it was designated as a [[Ken-sha]] (prefectural shrine).
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated in the western part of Tsuyama City, with the sacred Mount Kami to the northwest and the Sarayama hills of Kume to the south, overlooking the Yoshii River. It is one of the three major shrines of Mimasaka Province, alongside {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}} and {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}.<ref>The other two major shrines of Mimasaka are Nakayama Shrine and Mimasaka Sōja Shrine.</ref>
Since the medieval period, the shrine enjoyed reverence from local lords, including the daimyō of the {{ill|Tsuyama Domain|ja|津山藩}}, the Mori family. The current main shrine, reconstructed by the Mori clan during the Edo period, is a designated Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture. The shrine also houses national-level Important Cultural Properties, such as statues of Zuishin (guardian deities).
== Deities ==
; Primary deity * '''Hiko Nagisa Take Uga Fuki Aezunomikoto''' (彦波限建鵜葺草葺不合尊) – The main enshrined deity.
; Associated deities * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} – Deity of {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}. * {{ill|Kagami Tsukuri no Mikoto|ja|鏡作命}} – Deity of {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}}, the Ichinomiya of Mimasaka Province.
== History ==
=== Foundation === According to shrine tradition, worship began at the Onokoro Iwa (碫馭盧岩) on the Yoshii River, which was regarded as a sacred rock (iwakura). The shrine itself was established during the second year of Emperor Ankan’s reign (534 CE) and subsequently moved to its current location.<ref name="origin">[http://space.geocities.jp/ninomiya_takano/contents/rekishi/yuisho/yuisho.html Takano Shrine History] (official site).</ref>
=== Development === The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a Shikinaisha of Mimasaka Province. Some theories identify the shrine in question as Takano Shrine in Ninomiya, while others propose the {{ill|Takano Shrine (Takahon-gō)|ja|高野神社 (津山市高野本郷)}} in Takahon-gō. Both remain disputed.
The shrine gained prominence in the Kamakura period, receiving patronage from notable figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, {{ill|Yamana Tokiharu|ja|山名時春}}, {{ill|Mōri Motonari|ja|毛利元就}}, and {{ill|Kobayakawa Takakage|ja|小早川隆景}}. In the Edo period, the Mori family, daimyō of the Tsuyama Domain, rebuilt the shrine. Later, the Matsudaira clan, successors to the domain, also supported the shrine.
During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was classified as a [[Ken-sha]] under the modern shrine ranking system.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Nationally Designated Important Cultural Properties === * '''Wooden Inscription Plaque''' – Designated August 2, 1901. * '''Wooden Zuishin Statues (2 figures)''' – Crafted in 1162. Designated June 28, 1956. * '''Wooden Lion Statues (2 pairs)''' – Dated to the 10th and 12th centuries. Considered among the oldest examples of this type in Japan. Designated June 8, 2004.<ref>“Newly Designated Cultural Properties,” *Monthly Cultural Properties*, Issue 489 (2004), pp. 24–25.</ref>
=== Prefectural and Municipal Designations === * '''Main Shrine''' – Prefectural designation in 1983. * '''Zuishin Gate and Roof Covering''' – Municipal designation in 1999. * '''Mukuno Tree in Unate Forest''' – Municipal designation in 1973.
== Festivals ==
* '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Summer Festival''' (July 18) * '''Autumn Festival''' (Sunday closest to October 22)
== Access == ; Address * 601 Ninomiya, Tsuyama City, Okayama Prefecture
; Transportation * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Innosho Station|ja|院庄駅}} on the {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Kishin Line|ja|姫新線}}.
== Surrounding Area == * '''Miwayama Burial Mounds''' – The largest keyhole-shaped burial mounds in Mimasaka. * '''Sakaku Shrine'''

'''Takano Shrine''' (高野神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tsuyama City|ja|津山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), a disputed {{ill|Ichinomiya|ja|一宮}} of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}}. Under the modern shrine ranking system, it was designated as a [[Ken-sha]] (prefectural shrine).
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated in the western part of Tsuyama City, with the sacred Mount Kami to the northwest and the Sarayama hills of Kume to the south, overlooking the Yoshii River. It is one of the three major shrines of Mimasaka Province, alongside {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}} and {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}.<ref>The other two major shrines of Mimasaka are Nakayama Shrine and Mimasaka Sōja Shrine.</ref>
Since the medieval period, the shrine enjoyed reverence from local lords, including the daimyō of the {{ill|Tsuyama Domain|ja|津山藩}}, the Mori family. The current main shrine, reconstructed by the Mori clan during the Edo period, is a designated Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture. The shrine also houses national-level Important Cultural Properties, such as statues of Zuishin (guardian deities).
== Deities ==
; Primary deity * '''Hiko Nagisa Take Uga Fuki Aezunomikoto''' (彦波限建鵜葺草葺不合尊) – The main enshrined deity.
; Associated deities * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} – Deity of {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}. * {{ill|Kagami Tsukuri no Mikoto|ja|鏡作命}} – Deity of {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}}, the Ichinomiya of Mimasaka Province.
== History ==
=== Foundation === According to shrine tradition, worship began at the Onokoro Iwa (碫馭盧岩) on the Yoshii River, which was regarded as a sacred rock (iwakura). The shrine itself was established during the second year of Emperor Ankan’s reign (534 CE) and subsequently moved to its current location.<ref name="origin">[http://space.geocities.jp/ninomiya_takano/contents/rekishi/yuisho/yuisho.html Takano Shrine History] (official site).</ref>
=== Development === The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a Shikinaisha of Mimasaka Province. Some theories identify the shrine in question as Takano Shrine in Ninomiya, while others propose the {{ill|Takano Shrine (Takahon-gō)|ja|高野神社 (津山市高野本郷)}} in Takahon-gō. Both remain disputed.
The shrine gained prominence in the Kamakura period, receiving patronage from notable figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, {{ill|Yamana Tokiharu|ja|山名時春}}, {{ill|Mōri Motonari|ja|毛利元就}}, and {{ill|Kobayakawa Takakage|ja|小早川隆景}}. In the Edo period, the Mori family, daimyō of the Tsuyama Domain, rebuilt the shrine. Later, the Matsudaira clan, successors to the domain, also supported the shrine.
During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was classified as a [[Ken-sha]] under the modern shrine ranking system.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Nationally Designated Important Cultural Properties === * '''Wooden Inscription Plaque''' – Designated August 2, 1901. * '''Wooden Zuishin Statues (2 figures)''' – Crafted in 1162. Designated June 28, 1956. * '''Wooden Lion Statues (2 pairs)''' – Dated to the 10th and 12th centuries. Considered among the oldest examples of this type in Japan. Designated June 8, 2004.<ref>“Newly Designated Cultural Properties,” *Monthly Cultural Properties*, Issue 489 (2004), pp. 24–25.</ref>
=== Prefectural and Municipal Designations === * '''Main Shrine''' – Prefectural designation in 1983. * '''Zuishin Gate and Roof Covering''' – Municipal designation in 1999. * '''Mukuno Tree in Unate Forest''' – Municipal designation in 1973.
== Festivals ==
* '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Summer Festival''' (July 18) * '''Autumn Festival''' (Sunday closest to October 22)
== Access == ; Address * 601 Ninomiya, Tsuyama City, Okayama Prefecture
; Transportation * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Innosho Station|ja|院庄駅}} on the {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Kishin Line|ja|姫新線}}.
== Surrounding Area == * '''Miwayama Burial Mounds''' – The largest keyhole-shaped burial mounds in Mimasaka. * '''Sakaku Shrine'''
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://space.geocities.jp/ninomiya_takano/top/menu.html Takano Shrine Official Website (archived)] * [http://www.city.tsuyama.lg.jp/index.cfm/22,5208,116,371,html Overview of Takano Shrine Cultural Properties (Tsuyama City Website)] * [http://www.okayama-jinjacho.or.jp/cgi-bin/jsearch.cgi?mode=detail&jcode=03005 Takano Shrine (Okayama Prefecture Shrine Association)]

=== Development === The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a Shikinaisha of Mimasaka Province. Some theories identify the shrine in question as Takano Shrine in Ninomiya, while others propose the {{ill|Takano Shrine (Takahon-gō)|ja|高野神社 (津山市高野本郷)}} in Takahon-gō. Both remain disputed.
The shrine gained prominence in the Kamakura period, receiving patronage from notable figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, {{ill|Yamana Tokiharu|ja|山名時春}}, {{ill|Mōri Motonari|ja|毛利元就}}, and {{ill|Kobayakawa Takakage|ja|小早川隆景}}. In the Edo period, the Mori family, daimyō of the Tsuyama Domain, rebuilt the shrine. Later, the Matsudaira clan, successors to the domain, also supported the shrine.
During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was classified as a [[Ken-sha]] under the modern shrine ranking system.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Nationally Designated Important Cultural Properties === * '''Wooden Inscription Plaque''' – Designated August 2, 1901. * '''Wooden Zuishin Statues (2 figures)''' – Crafted in 1162. Designated June 28, 1956. * '''Wooden Lion Statues (2 pairs)''' – Dated to the 10th and 12th centuries. Considered among the oldest examples of this type in Japan. Designated June 8, 2004.<ref>“Newly Designated Cultural Properties,” *Monthly Cultural Properties*, Issue 489 (2004), pp. 24–25.</ref>
=== Prefectural and Municipal Designations === * '''Main Shrine''' – Prefectural designation in 1983. * '''Zuishin Gate and Roof Covering''' – Municipal designation in 1999. * '''Mukuno Tree in Unate Forest''' – Municipal designation in 1973.
== Festivals ==
* '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Summer Festival''' (July 18) * '''Autumn Festival''' (Sunday closest to October 22)

'''Takano Shrine''' (高野神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tsuyama City|ja|津山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), a disputed {{ill|Ichinomiya|ja|一宮}} of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}}. Under the modern shrine ranking system, it was designated as a [[Ken-sha]] (prefectural shrine).
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated in the western part of Tsuyama City, with the sacred Mount Kami to the northwest and the Sarayama hills of Kume to the south, overlooking the Yoshii River. It is one of the three major shrines of Mimasaka Province, alongside {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}} and {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}.<ref>The other two major shrines of Mimasaka are Nakayama Shrine and Mimasaka Sōja Shrine.</ref>
Since the medieval period, the shrine enjoyed reverence from local lords, including the daimyō of the {{ill|Tsuyama Domain|ja|津山藩}}, the Mori family. The current main shrine, reconstructed by the Mori clan during the Edo period, is a designated Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture. The shrine also houses national-level Important Cultural Properties, such as statues of Zuishin (guardian deities).
== Deities ==
; Primary deity * '''Hiko Nagisa Take Uga Fuki Aezunomikoto''' (彦波限建鵜葺草葺不合尊) – The main enshrined deity.
; Associated deities * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} – Deity of {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}. * {{ill|Kagami Tsukuri no Mikoto|ja|鏡作命}} – Deity of {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}}, the Ichinomiya of Mimasaka Province.
== History ==
=== Foundation === According to shrine tradition, worship began at the Onokoro Iwa (碫馭盧岩) on the Yoshii River, which was regarded as a sacred rock (iwakura). The shrine itself was established during the second year of Emperor Ankan’s reign (534 CE) and subsequently moved to its current location.<ref name="origin">[http://space.geocities.jp/ninomiya_takano/contents/rekishi/yuisho/yuisho.html Takano Shrine History] (official site).</ref>
=== Development === The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a Shikinaisha of Mimasaka Province. Some theories identify the shrine in question as Takano Shrine in Ninomiya, while others propose the {{ill|Takano Shrine (Takahon-gō)|ja|高野神社 (津山市高野本郷)}} in Takahon-gō. Both remain disputed.
The shrine gained prominence in the Kamakura period, receiving patronage from notable figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, {{ill|Yamana Tokiharu|ja|山名時春}}, {{ill|Mōri Motonari|ja|毛利元就}}, and {{ill|Kobayakawa Takakage|ja|小早川隆景}}. In the Edo period, the Mori family, daimyō of the Tsuyama Domain, rebuilt the shrine. Later, the Matsudaira clan, successors to the domain, also supported the shrine.
During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was classified as a [[Ken-sha]] under the modern shrine ranking system.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Nationally Designated Important Cultural Properties === * '''Wooden Inscription Plaque''' – Designated August 2, 1901. * '''Wooden Zuishin Statues (2 figures)''' – Crafted in 1162. Designated June 28, 1956. * '''Wooden Lion Statues (2 pairs)''' – Dated to the 10th and 12th centuries. Considered among the oldest examples of this type in Japan. Designated June 8, 2004.<ref>“Newly Designated Cultural Properties,” *Monthly Cultural Properties*, Issue 489 (2004), pp. 24–25.</ref>
=== Prefectural and Municipal Designations === * '''Main Shrine''' – Prefectural designation in 1983. * '''Zuishin Gate and Roof Covering''' – Municipal designation in 1999. * '''Mukuno Tree in Unate Forest''' – Municipal designation in 1973.
== Festivals ==
* '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Summer Festival''' (July 18) * '''Autumn Festival''' (Sunday closest to October 22)

'''Takano Shrine''' (高野神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tsuyama City|ja|津山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), a disputed {{ill|Ichinomiya|ja|一宮}} of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}}. Under the modern shrine ranking system, it was designated as a [[Ken-sha]] (prefectural shrine).
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated in the western part of Tsuyama City, with the sacred Mount Kami to the northwest and the Sarayama hills of Kume to the south, overlooking the Yoshii River. It is one of the three major shrines of Mimasaka Province, alongside {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}} and {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}.<ref>The other two major shrines of Mimasaka are Nakayama Shrine and Mimasaka Sōja Shrine.</ref>
Since the medieval period, the shrine enjoyed reverence from local lords, including the daimyō of the {{ill|Tsuyama Domain|ja|津山藩}}, the Mori family. The current main shrine, reconstructed by the Mori clan during the Edo period, is a designated Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture. The shrine also houses national-level Important Cultural Properties, such as statues of Zuishin (guardian deities).
== Deities ==
; Primary deity * '''Hiko Nagisa Take Uga Fuki Aezunomikoto''' (彦波限建鵜葺草葺不合尊) – The main enshrined deity.
; Associated deities * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} – Deity of {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}. * {{ill|Kagami Tsukuri no Mikoto|ja|鏡作命}} – Deity of {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}}, the Ichinomiya of Mimasaka Province.
== History ==
=== Foundation === According to shrine tradition, worship began at the Onokoro Iwa (碫馭盧岩) on the Yoshii River, which was regarded as a sacred rock (iwakura). The shrine itself was established during the second year of Emperor Ankan’s reign (534 CE) and subsequently moved to its current location.<ref name="origin">[http://space.geocities.jp/ninomiya_takano/contents/rekishi/yuisho/yuisho.html Takano Shrine History] (official site).</ref>
=== Development === The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a Shikinaisha of Mimasaka Province. Some theories identify the shrine in question as Takano Shrine in Ninomiya, while others propose the {{ill|Takano Shrine (Takahon-gō)|ja|高野神社 (津山市高野本郷)}} in Takahon-gō. Both remain disputed.
The shrine gained prominence in the Kamakura period, receiving patronage from notable figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, {{ill|Yamana Tokiharu|ja|山名時春}}, {{ill|Mōri Motonari|ja|毛利元就}}, and {{ill|Kobayakawa Takakage|ja|小早川隆景}}. In the Edo period, the Mori family, daimyō of the Tsuyama Domain, rebuilt the shrine. Later, the Matsudaira clan, successors to the domain, also supported the shrine.
During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was classified as a [[Ken-sha]] under the modern shrine ranking system.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Nationally Designated Important Cultural Properties === * '''Wooden Inscription Plaque''' – Designated August 2, 1901. * '''Wooden Zuishin Statues (2 figures)''' – Crafted in 1162. Designated June 28, 1956. * '''Wooden Lion Statues (2 pairs)''' – Dated to the 10th and 12th centuries. Considered among the oldest examples of this type in Japan. Designated June 8, 2004.<ref>“Newly Designated Cultural Properties,” *Monthly Cultural Properties*, Issue 489 (2004), pp. 24–25.</ref>
=== Prefectural and Municipal Designations === * '''Main Shrine''' – Prefectural designation in 1983. * '''Zuishin Gate and Roof Covering''' – Municipal designation in 1999. * '''Mukuno Tree in Unate Forest''' – Municipal designation in 1973.
== Festivals ==
* '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Summer Festival''' (July 18) * '''Autumn Festival''' (Sunday closest to October 22)
== Access == ; Address * 601 Ninomiya, Tsuyama City, Okayama Prefecture
; Transportation * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Innosho Station|ja|院庄駅}} on the {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Kishin Line|ja|姫新線}}.
== Surrounding Area == * '''Miwayama Kofuns''' – The largest keyhole-shaped Kofuns in Mimasaka. * '''Sakaku Shrine'''

'''Takano Shrine''' (高野神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tsuyama City|ja|津山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), a disputed {{ill|Ichinomiya|ja|一宮}} of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}}. Under the modern shrine ranking system, it was designated as a [[Ken-sha]] (prefectural shrine).
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated in the western part of Tsuyama City, with the sacred Mount Kami to the northwest and the Sarayama hills of Kume to the south, overlooking the Yoshii River. It is one of the three major shrines of Mimasaka Province, alongside {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}} and {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}.<ref>The other two major shrines of Mimasaka are Nakayama Shrine and Mimasaka Sōja Shrine.</ref>
Since the medieval period, the shrine enjoyed reverence from local lords, including the daimyō of the {{ill|Tsuyama Domain|ja|津山藩}}, the Mori family. The current main shrine, reconstructed by the Mori clan during the Edo period, is a designated Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture. The shrine also houses national-level Important Cultural Properties, such as statues of Zuishin (guardian deities).
== Deities ==
; Primary deity * '''Hiko Nagisa Take Uga Fuki Aezunomikoto''' (彦波限建鵜葺草葺不合尊) – The main enshrined deity.
; Associated deities * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} – Deity of {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}. * {{ill|Kagami Tsukuri no Mikoto|ja|鏡作命}} – Deity of {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}}, the Ichinomiya of Mimasaka Province.
== History ==
=== Foundation === According to shrine tradition, worship began at the Onokoro Iwa (碫馭盧岩) on the Yoshii River, which was regarded as a sacred rock (iwakura). The shrine itself was established during the second year of Emperor Ankan’s reign (534 CE) and subsequently moved to its current location.<ref name="origin">[http://space.geocities.jp/ninomiya_takano/contents/rekishi/yuisho/yuisho.html Takano Shrine History] (official site).</ref>
=== Development === The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a Shikinaisha of Mimasaka Province. Some theories identify the shrine in question as Takano Shrine in Ninomiya, while others propose the {{ill|Takano Shrine (Takahon-gō)|ja|高野神社 (津山市高野本郷)}} in Takahon-gō. Both remain disputed.
The shrine gained prominence in the Kamakura period, receiving patronage from notable figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, {{ill|Yamana Tokiharu|ja|山名時春}}, {{ill|Mōri Motonari|ja|毛利元就}}, and {{ill|Kobayakawa Takakage|ja|小早川隆景}}. In the Edo period, the Mori family, daimyō of the Tsuyama Domain, rebuilt the shrine. Later, the Matsudaira clan, successors to the domain, also supported the shrine.
During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was classified as a [[Ken-sha]] under the modern shrine ranking system.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Nationally Designated Important Cultural Properties === * '''Wooden Inscription Plaque''' – Designated August 2, 1901. * '''Wooden Zuishin Statues (2 figures)''' – Crafted in 1162. Designated June 28, 1956. * '''Wooden Lion Statues (2 pairs)''' – Dated to the 10th and 12th centuries. Considered among the oldest examples of this type in Japan. Designated June 8, 2004.<ref>“Newly Designated Cultural Properties,” *Monthly Cultural Properties*, Issue 489 (2004), pp. 24–25.</ref>
=== Prefectural and Municipal Designations === * '''Main Shrine''' – Prefectural designation in 1983. * '''Zuishin Gate and Roof Covering''' – Municipal designation in 1999. * '''Mukuno Tree in Unate Forest''' – Municipal designation in 1973.
== Festivals ==
* '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Summer Festival''' (July 18) * '''Autumn Festival''' (Sunday closest to October 22)
== Access == ; Address * 601 Ninomiya, Tsuyama City, Okayama Prefecture
; Transportation * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Innosho Station|ja|院庄駅}} on the {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Kishin Line|ja|姫新線}}.
== Surrounding Area == * '''Miwayama Kofuns''' – The largest keyhole-shaped Kofuns in Mimasaka. * '''Sakaku Shrine'''

'''Takano Shrine''' (高野神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tsuyama City|ja|津山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), a disputed {{ill|Ichinomiya|ja|一宮}} of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}}. Under the modern shrine ranking system, it was designated as a [[Ken-sha]] (prefectural shrine).
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated in the western part of Tsuyama City, with the sacred Mount Kami to the northwest and the Sarayama hills of Kume to the south, overlooking the Yoshii River. It is one of the three major shrines of Mimasaka Province, alongside {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}} and {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}.<ref>The other two major shrines of Mimasaka are Nakayama Shrine and Mimasaka Sōja Shrine.</ref>
Since the medieval period, the shrine enjoyed reverence from local lords, including the daimyō of the {{ill|Tsuyama Domain|ja|津山藩}}, the Mori family. The current main shrine, reconstructed by the Mori clan during the Edo period, is a designated Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture. The shrine also houses national-level Important Cultural Properties, such as statues of Zuishin (guardian deities).
== Deities ==
; Primary deity * '''Hiko Nagisa Take Uga Fuki Aezunomikoto''' (彦波限建鵜葺草葺不合尊) – The main enshrined deity.
; Associated deities * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} – Deity of {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}. * {{ill|Kagami Tsukuri no Mikoto|ja|鏡作命}} – Deity of {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}}, the Ichinomiya of Mimasaka Province.
== History ==
=== Foundation === According to shrine tradition, worship began at the Onokoro Iwa (碫馭盧岩) on the Yoshii River, which was regarded as a sacred rock (iwakura). The shrine itself was established during the second year of Emperor Ankan’s reign (534 CE) and subsequently moved to its current location.<ref name="origin">[http://space.geocities.jp/ninomiya_takano/contents/rekishi/yuisho/yuisho.html Takano Shrine History] (official site).</ref>
=== Development === The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a Shikinaisha of Mimasaka Province. Some theories identify the shrine in question as Takano Shrine in Ninomiya, while others propose the {{ill|Takano Shrine (Takahon-gō)|ja|高野神社 (津山市高野本郷)}} in Takahon-gō. Both remain disputed.
The shrine gained prominence in the Kamakura period, receiving patronage from notable figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, {{ill|Yamana Tokiharu|ja|山名時春}}, {{ill|Mōri Motonari|ja|毛利元就}}, and {{ill|Kobayakawa Takakage|ja|小早川隆景}}. In the Edo period, the Mori family, daimyō of the Tsuyama Domain, rebuilt the shrine. Later, the Matsudaira clan, successors to the domain, also supported the shrine.
During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was classified as a [[Ken-sha]] under the modern shrine ranking system.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Nationally Designated Important Cultural Properties === * '''Wooden Inscription Plaque''' – Designated August 2, 1901. * '''Wooden Zuishin Statues (2 figures)''' – Crafted in 1162. Designated June 28, 1956. * '''Wooden Lion Statues (2 pairs)''' – Dated to the 10th and 12th centuries. Considered among the oldest examples of this type in Japan. Designated June 8, 2004.<ref>“Newly Designated Cultural Properties,” *Monthly Cultural Properties*, Issue 489 (2004), pp. 24–25.</ref>
=== Prefectural and Municipal Designations === * '''Main Shrine''' – Prefectural designation in 1983. * '''Zuishin Gate and Roof Covering''' – Municipal designation in 1999. * '''Mukuno Tree in Unate Forest''' – Municipal designation in 1973.
== Festivals ==
* '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Summer Festival''' (July 18) * '''Autumn Festival''' (Sunday closest to October 22)
== Access == ; Address * 601 Ninomiya, Tsuyama City, Okayama Prefecture
; Transportation * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Innosho Station|ja|院庄駅}} on the {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Kishin Line|ja|姫新線}}.
== Surrounding Area == * '''Miwayama Kofuns''' – The largest keyhole-shaped Kofuns in Mimasaka. * '''Sakaku Shrine'''

'''Takano Shrine''' (高野神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tsuyama City|ja|津山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), a disputed {{ill|Ichinomiya|ja|一宮}} of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}}. Under the modern shrine ranking system, it was designated as a [[Ken-sha]] (prefectural shrine).
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated in the western part of Tsuyama City, with the sacred Mount Kami to the northwest and the Sarayama hills of Kume to the south, overlooking the Yoshii River. It is one of the three major shrines of Mimasaka Province, alongside {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}} and {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}.<ref>The other two major shrines of Mimasaka are Nakayama Shrine and Mimasaka Sōja Shrine.</ref>
Since the medieval period, the shrine enjoyed reverence from local lords, including the daimyō of the {{ill|Tsuyama Domain|ja|津山藩}}, the Mori family. The current main shrine, reconstructed by the Mori clan during the Edo period, is a designated Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture. The shrine also houses national-level Important Cultural Properties, such as statues of Zuishin (guardian deities).
== Deities ==
; Primary deity * '''Hiko Nagisa Take Uga Fuki Aezunomikoto''' (彦波限建鵜葺草葺不合尊) – The main enshrined deity.
; Associated deities * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} – Deity of {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}. * {{ill|Kagami Tsukuri no Mikoto|ja|鏡作命}} – Deity of {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}}, the Ichinomiya of Mimasaka Province.
== History ==
=== Foundation === According to shrine tradition, worship began at the Onokoro Iwa (碫馭盧岩) on the Yoshii River, which was regarded as a sacred rock (iwakura). The shrine itself was established during the second year of Emperor Ankan’s reign (534 CE) and subsequently moved to its current location.<ref name="origin">[http://space.geocities.jp/ninomiya_takano/contents/rekishi/yuisho/yuisho.html Takano Shrine History] (official site).</ref>
=== Development === The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a Shikinaisha of Mimasaka Province. Some theories identify the shrine in question as Takano Shrine in Ninomiya, while others propose the {{ill|Takano Shrine (Takahon-gō)|ja|高野神社 (津山市高野本郷)}} in Takahon-gō. Both remain disputed.
The shrine gained prominence in the Kamakura period, receiving patronage from notable figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, {{ill|Yamana Tokiharu|ja|山名時春}}, {{ill|Mōri Motonari|ja|毛利元就}}, and {{ill|Kobayakawa Takakage|ja|小早川隆景}}. In the Edo period, the Mori family, daimyō of the Tsuyama Domain, rebuilt the shrine. Later, the Matsudaira clan, successors to the domain, also supported the shrine.
During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was classified as a [[Ken-sha]] under the modern shrine ranking system.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Nationally Designated Important Cultural Properties === * '''Wooden Inscription Plaque''' – Designated August 2, 1901. * '''Wooden Zuishin Statues (2 figures)''' – Crafted in 1162. Designated June 28, 1956. * '''Wooden Lion Statues (2 pairs)''' – Dated to the 10th and 12th centuries. Considered among the oldest examples of this type in Japan. Designated June 8, 2004.<ref>“Newly Designated Cultural Properties,” *Monthly Cultural Properties*, Issue 489 (2004), pp. 24–25.</ref>
=== Prefectural and Municipal Designations === * '''Main Shrine''' – Prefectural designation in 1983. * '''Zuishin Gate and Roof Covering''' – Municipal designation in 1999. * '''Mukuno Tree in Unate Forest''' – Municipal designation in 1973.
== Festivals ==
* '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Summer Festival''' (July 18) * '''Autumn Festival''' (Sunday closest to October 22)
== Access == ; Address * 601 Ninomiya, Tsuyama City, Okayama Prefecture
; Transportation * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Innosho Station|ja|院庄駅}} on the {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Kishin Line|ja|姫新線}}.
== Surrounding Area == * '''Miwayama Kofuns''' – The largest keyhole-shaped Kofuns in Mimasaka. * '''Sakaku Shrine'''

'''Takano Shrine''' (高野神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tsuyama City|ja|津山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), a disputed {{ill|Ichinomiya|ja|一宮}} of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}}. Under the modern shrine ranking system, it was designated as a [[Ken-sha]] (prefectural shrine).
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated in the western part of Tsuyama City, with the sacred Mount Kami to the northwest and the Sarayama hills of Kume to the south, overlooking the Yoshii River. It is one of the three major shrines of Mimasaka Province, alongside {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}} and {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}.<ref>The other two major shrines of Mimasaka are Nakayama Shrine and Mimasaka Sōja Shrine.</ref>
Since the medieval period, the shrine enjoyed reverence from local lords, including the daimyō of the {{ill|Tsuyama Domain|ja|津山藩}}, the Mori family. The current main shrine, reconstructed by the Mori clan during the Edo period, is a designated Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture. The shrine also houses national-level Important Cultural Properties, such as statues of Zuishin (guardian deities).
== Deities ==
; Primary deity * '''Hiko Nagisa Take Uga Fuki Aezunomikoto''' (彦波限建鵜葺草葺不合尊) – The main enshrined deity.
; Associated deities * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} – Deity of {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}. * {{ill|Kagami Tsukuri no Mikoto|ja|鏡作命}} – Deity of {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}}, the Ichinomiya of Mimasaka Province.
== History ==
=== Foundation === According to shrine tradition, worship began at the Onokoro Iwa (碫馭盧岩) on the Yoshii River, which was regarded as a sacred rock (iwakura). The shrine itself was established during the second year of Emperor Ankan’s reign (534 CE) and subsequently moved to its current location.<ref name="origin">[http://space.geocities.jp/ninomiya_takano/contents/rekishi/yuisho/yuisho.html Takano Shrine History] (official site).</ref>
=== Development === The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a Shikinaisha of Mimasaka Province. Some theories identify the shrine in question as Takano Shrine in Ninomiya, while others propose the {{ill|Takano Shrine (Takahon-gō)|ja|高野神社 (津山市高野本郷)}} in Takahon-gō. Both remain disputed.
The shrine gained prominence in the Kamakura period, receiving patronage from notable figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, {{ill|Yamana Tokiharu|ja|山名時春}}, {{ill|Mōri Motonari|ja|毛利元就}}, and {{ill|Kobayakawa Takakage|ja|小早川隆景}}. In the Edo period, the Mori family, daimyō of the Tsuyama Domain, rebuilt the shrine. Later, the Matsudaira clan, successors to the domain, also supported the shrine.
During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was classified as a [[Ken-sha]] under the modern shrine ranking system.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Nationally Designated Important Cultural Properties === * '''Wooden Inscription Plaque''' – Designated August 2, 1901. * '''Wooden Zuishin Statues (2 figures)''' – Crafted in 1162. Designated June 28, 1956. * '''Wooden Lion Statues (2 pairs)''' – Dated to the 10th and 12th centuries. Considered among the oldest examples of this type in Japan. Designated June 8, 2004.<ref>“Newly Designated Cultural Properties,” *Monthly Cultural Properties*, Issue 489 (2004), pp. 24–25.</ref>
=== Prefectural and Municipal Designations === * '''Main Shrine''' – Prefectural designation in 1983. * '''Zuishin Gate and Roof Covering''' – Municipal designation in 1999. * '''Mukuno Tree in Unate Forest''' – Municipal designation in 1973.
== Festivals ==
* '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Summer Festival''' (July 18) * '''Autumn Festival''' (Sunday closest to October 22)

'''Takano Shrine''' (高野神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tsuyama City|ja|津山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), a disputed {{ill|Ichinomiya|ja|一宮}} of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}}. Under the modern shrine ranking system, it was designated as a [[Ken-sha]] (prefectural shrine).
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated in the western part of Tsuyama City, with the sacred Mount Kami to the northwest and the Sarayama hills of Kume to the south, overlooking the Yoshii River. It is one of the three major shrines of Mimasaka Province, alongside {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}} and {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}.<ref>The other two major shrines of Mimasaka are Nakayama Shrine and Mimasaka Sōja Shrine.</ref>
Since the medieval period, the shrine enjoyed reverence from local lords, including the daimyō of the {{ill|Tsuyama Domain|ja|津山藩}}, the Mori family. The current main shrine, reconstructed by the Mori clan during the Edo period, is a designated Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture. The shrine also houses national-level Important Cultural Properties, such as statues of Zuishin (guardian deities).
== Deities ==
; Primary deity * '''Hiko Nagisa Take Uga Fuki Aezunomikoto''' (彦波限建鵜葺草葺不合尊) – The main enshrined deity.
; Associated deities * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} – Deity of {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}. * {{ill|Kagami Tsukuri no Mikoto|ja|鏡作命}} – Deity of {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}}, the Ichinomiya of Mimasaka Province.
== History ==
=== Foundation === According to shrine tradition, worship began at the Onokoro Iwa (碫馭盧岩) on the Yoshii River, which was regarded as a sacred rock (iwakura). The shrine itself was established during the second year of Emperor Ankan’s reign (534 CE) and subsequently moved to its current location.<ref name="origin">[http://space.geocities.jp/ninomiya_takano/contents/rekishi/yuisho/yuisho.html Takano Shrine History] (official site).</ref>
=== Development === The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a Shikinaisha of Mimasaka Province. Some theories identify the shrine in question as Takano Shrine in Ninomiya, while others propose the {{ill|Takano Shrine (Takahon-gō)|ja|高野神社 (津山市高野本郷)}} in Takahon-gō. Both remain disputed.
The shrine gained prominence in the Kamakura period, receiving patronage from notable figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, {{ill|Yamana Tokiharu|ja|山名時春}}, {{ill|Mōri Motonari|ja|毛利元就}}, and {{ill|Kobayakawa Takakage|ja|小早川隆景}}. In the Edo period, the Mori family, daimyō of the Tsuyama Domain, rebuilt the shrine. Later, the Matsudaira clan, successors to the domain, also supported the shrine.
During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was classified as a [[Ken-sha]] under the modern shrine ranking system.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Nationally Designated Important Cultural Properties === * '''Wooden Inscription Plaque''' – Designated August 2, 1901. * '''Wooden Zuishin Statues (2 figures)''' – Crafted in 1162. Designated June 28, 1956. * '''Wooden Lion Statues (2 pairs)''' – Dated to the 10th and 12th centuries. Considered among the oldest examples of this type in Japan. Designated June 8, 2004.<ref>“Newly Designated Cultural Properties,” *Monthly Cultural Properties*, Issue 489 (2004), pp. 24–25.</ref>
=== Prefectural and Municipal Designations === * '''Main Shrine''' – Prefectural designation in 1983. * '''Zuishin Gate and Roof Covering''' – Municipal designation in 1999. * '''Mukuno Tree in Unate Forest''' – Municipal designation in 1973.
== Festivals ==
* '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Summer Festival''' (July 18) * '''Autumn Festival''' (Sunday closest to October 22)
== Access == ; Address * 601 Ninomiya, Tsuyama City, Okayama Prefecture
; Transportation * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Innosho Station|ja|院庄駅}} on the {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Kishin Line|ja|姫新線}}.
== Surrounding Area == * '''Miwayama Kofuns''' – The largest keyhole-shaped Kofuns in Mimasaka. * '''Sakaku Shrine'''

'''Takano Shrine''' (高野神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tsuyama City|ja|津山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), a disputed {{ill|Ichinomiya|ja|一宮}} of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}}. Under the modern shrine ranking system, it was designated as a [[Ken-sha]] (prefectural shrine).
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated in the western part of Tsuyama City, with the sacred Mount Kami to the northwest and the Sarayama hills of Kume to the south, overlooking the Yoshii River. It is one of the three major shrines of Mimasaka Province, alongside {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}} and {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}.<ref>The other two major shrines of Mimasaka are Nakayama Shrine and Mimasaka Sōja Shrine.</ref>
Since the medieval period, the shrine enjoyed reverence from local lords, including the daimyō of the {{ill|Tsuyama Domain|ja|津山藩}}, the Mori family. The current main shrine, reconstructed by the Mori clan during the Edo period, is a designated Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture. The shrine also houses national-level Important Cultural Properties, such as statues of Zuishin (guardian deities).
== Deities ==
; Primary deity * '''Hiko Nagisa Take Uga Fuki Aezunomikoto''' (彦波限建鵜葺草葺不合尊) – The main enshrined deity.
; Associated deities * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} – Deity of {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}. * {{ill|Kagami Tsukuri no Mikoto|ja|鏡作命}} – Deity of {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}}, the Ichinomiya of Mimasaka Province.
== History ==
=== Foundation === According to shrine tradition, worship began at the Onokoro Iwa (碫馭盧岩) on the Yoshii River, which was regarded as a sacred rock (iwakura). The shrine itself was established during the second year of Emperor Ankan’s reign (534 CE) and subsequently moved to its current location.<ref name="origin">[http://space.geocities.jp/ninomiya_takano/contents/rekishi/yuisho/yuisho.html Takano Shrine History] (official site).</ref>
=== Development === The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a Shikinaisha of Mimasaka Province. Some theories identify the shrine in question as Takano Shrine in Ninomiya, while others propose the {{ill|Takano Shrine (Takahon-gō)|ja|高野神社 (津山市高野本郷)}} in Takahon-gō. Both remain disputed.
The shrine gained prominence in the Kamakura period, receiving patronage from notable figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, {{ill|Yamana Tokiharu|ja|山名時春}}, {{ill|Mōri Motonari|ja|毛利元就}}, and {{ill|Kobayakawa Takakage|ja|小早川隆景}}. In the Edo period, the Mori family, daimyō of the Tsuyama Domain, rebuilt the shrine. Later, the Matsudaira clan, successors to the domain, also supported the shrine.
During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was classified as a [[Ken-sha]] under the modern shrine ranking system.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Nationally Designated Important Cultural Properties === * '''Wooden Inscription Plaque''' – Designated August 2, 1901. * '''Wooden Zuishin Statues (2 figures)''' – Crafted in 1162. Designated June 28, 1956. * '''Wooden Lion Statues (2 pairs)''' – Dated to the 10th and 12th centuries. Considered among the oldest examples of this type in Japan. Designated June 8, 2004.<ref>“Newly Designated Cultural Properties,” *Monthly Cultural Properties*, Issue 489 (2004), pp. 24–25.</ref>
=== Prefectural and Municipal Designations === * '''Main Shrine''' – Prefectural designation in 1983. * '''Zuishin Gate and Roof Covering''' – Municipal designation in 1999. * '''Mukuno Tree in Unate Forest''' – Municipal designation in 1973.
== Festivals ==
* '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Summer Festival''' (July 18) * '''Autumn Festival''' (Sunday closest to October 22)
== Access == ; Address * 601 Ninomiya, Tsuyama City, Okayama Prefecture
; Transportation * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Innosho Station|ja|院庄駅}} on the {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Kishin Line|ja|姫新線}}.
== Surrounding Area == * '''Miwayama Kofuns''' – The largest keyhole-shaped Kofuns in Mimasaka. * '''Sakaku Shrine'''

'''Takano Shrine''' (高野神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tsuyama City|ja|津山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), a disputed {{ill|Ichinomiya|ja|一宮}} of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}}. Under the modern shrine ranking system, it was designated as a [[Ken-sha]] (prefectural shrine).
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated in the western part of Tsuyama City, with the sacred Mount Kami to the northwest and the Sarayama hills of Kume to the south, overlooking the Yoshii River. It is one of the three major shrines of Mimasaka Province, alongside {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}} and {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}.<ref>The other two major shrines of Mimasaka are Nakayama Shrine and Mimasaka Sōja Shrine.</ref>
Since the medieval period, the shrine enjoyed reverence from local lords, including the daimyō of the {{ill|Tsuyama Domain|ja|津山藩}}, the Mori family. The current main shrine, reconstructed by the Mori clan during the Edo period, is a designated Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture. The shrine also houses national-level Important Cultural Properties, such as statues of Zuishin (guardian deities).
== Deities ==
; Primary deity * '''Hiko Nagisa Take Uga Fuki Aezunomikoto''' (彦波限建鵜葺草葺不合尊) – The main enshrined deity.
; Associated deities * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} – Deity of {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}. * {{ill|Kagami Tsukuri no Mikoto|ja|鏡作命}} – Deity of {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}}, the Ichinomiya of Mimasaka Province.
== History ==
=== Foundation === According to shrine tradition, worship began at the Onokoro Iwa (碫馭盧岩) on the Yoshii River, which was regarded as a sacred rock (iwakura). The shrine itself was established during the second year of Emperor Ankan’s reign (534 CE) and subsequently moved to its current location.<ref name="origin">[http://space.geocities.jp/ninomiya_takano/contents/rekishi/yuisho/yuisho.html Takano Shrine History] (official site).</ref>
=== Development === The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a Shikinaisha of Mimasaka Province. Some theories identify the shrine in question as Takano Shrine in Ninomiya, while others propose the {{ill|Takano Shrine (Takahon-gō)|ja|高野神社 (津山市高野本郷)}} in Takahon-gō. Both remain disputed.
The shrine gained prominence in the Kamakura period, receiving patronage from notable figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, {{ill|Yamana Tokiharu|ja|山名時春}}, {{ill|Mōri Motonari|ja|毛利元就}}, and {{ill|Kobayakawa Takakage|ja|小早川隆景}}. In the Edo period, the Mori family, daimyō of the Tsuyama Domain, rebuilt the shrine. Later, the Matsudaira clan, successors to the domain, also supported the shrine.
During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was classified as a [[Ken-sha]] under the modern shrine ranking system.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Nationally Designated Important Cultural Properties === * '''Wooden Inscription Plaque''' – Designated August 2, 1901. * '''Wooden Zuishin Statues (2 figures)''' – Crafted in 1162. Designated June 28, 1956. * '''Wooden Lion Statues (2 pairs)''' – Dated to the 10th and 12th centuries. Considered among the oldest examples of this type in Japan. Designated June 8, 2004.<ref>“Newly Designated Cultural Properties,” *Monthly Cultural Properties*, Issue 489 (2004), pp. 24–25.</ref>
=== Prefectural and Municipal Designations === * '''Main Shrine''' – Prefectural designation in 1983. * '''Zuishin Gate and Roof Covering''' – Municipal designation in 1999. * '''Mukuno Tree in Unate Forest''' – Municipal designation in 1973.
== Festivals ==
* '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Summer Festival''' (July 18) * '''Autumn Festival''' (Sunday closest to October 22)
== Access == ; Address * 601 Ninomiya, Tsuyama City, Okayama Prefecture
; Transportation * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Innosho Station|ja|院庄駅}} on the {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Kishin Line|ja|姫新線}}.
== Surrounding Area == * '''Miwayama Kofuns''' – The largest keyhole-shaped Kofuns in Mimasaka. * '''Sakaku Shrine'''

The shrine itself was established during the second year of Emperor Ankan’s reign (534 CE) and subsequently moved to its current location.<ref name="origin">[http://space.geocities.jp/ninomiya_takano/contents/rekishi/yuisho/yuisho.html Takano Shrine History] (official site).</ref>
=== Development === The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a Shikinaisha of Mimasaka Province. Some theories identify the shrine in question as Takano Shrine in Ninomiya, while others propose the {{ill|Takano Shrine (Takahon-gō)|ja|高野神社 (津山市高野本郷)}} in Takahon-gō. Both remain disputed.
The shrine gained prominence in the Kamakura period, receiving patronage from notable figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, {{ill|Yamana Tokiharu|ja|山名時春}}, {{ill|Mōri Motonari|ja|毛利元就}}, and {{ill|Kobayakawa Takakage|ja|小早川隆景}}. In the Edo period, the Mori family, daimyō of the Tsuyama Domain, rebuilt the shrine. Later, the Matsudaira clan, successors to the domain, also supported the shrine.
During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was classified as a [[Ken-sha]] under the modern shrine ranking system.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Nationally Designated Important Cultural Properties === * '''Wooden Inscription Plaque''' – Designated August 2, 1901. * '''Wooden Zuishin Statues (2 figures)''' – Crafted in 1162. Designated June 28, 1956. * '''Wooden Lion Statues (2 pairs)''' – Dated to the 10th and 12th centuries. Considered among the oldest examples of this type in Japan. Designated June 8, 2004.<ref>“Newly Designated Cultural Properties,” *Monthly Cultural Properties*, Issue 489 (2004), pp. 24–25.</ref>
=== Prefectural and Municipal Designations === * '''Main Shrine''' – Prefectural designation in 1983. * '''Zuishin Gate and Roof Covering''' – Municipal designation in 1999. * '''Mukuno Tree in Unate Forest''' – Municipal designation in 1973.
== Festivals ==
* '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Summer Festival''' (July 18) * '''Autumn Festival''' (Sunday closest to October 22)
== Access == ; Address * 601 Ninomiya, Tsuyama City, Okayama Prefecture
; Transportation * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Innosho Station|ja|院庄駅}} on the {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Kishin Line|ja|姫新線}}.
== Surrounding Area == * '''Miwayama Kofuns''' – The largest keyhole-shaped Kofuns in Mimasaka. * '''Sakaku Shrine'''

revision.text:
'''Takano Shrine''' (高野神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tsuyama City|ja|津山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), a disputed {{ill|Ichinomiya|ja|一宮}} of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}}. Under the modern shrine ranking system, it was designated as a [[Ken-sha]] (prefectural shrine).
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated in the western part of Tsuyama City, with the sacred Mount Kami to the northwest and the Sarayama hills of Kume to the south, overlooking the Yoshii River. It is one of the three major shrines of Mimasaka Province, alongside {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}} and {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}.<ref>The other two major shrines of Mimasaka are Nakayama Shrine and Mimasaka Sōja Shrine.</ref>
Since the medieval period, the shrine enjoyed reverence from local lords, including the daimyō of the {{ill|Tsuyama Domain|ja|津山藩}}, the Mori family. The current main shrine, reconstructed by the Mori clan during the Edo period, is a designated Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture. The shrine also houses national-level Important Cultural Properties, such as statues of Zuishin (guardian deities).
== Deities ==
; Primary deity * '''Hiko Nagisa Take Uga Fuki Aezunomikoto''' (彦波限建鵜葺草葺不合尊) – The main enshrined deity.
; Associated deities * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} – Deity of {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}. * {{ill|Kagami Tsukuri no Mikoto|ja|鏡作命}} – Deity of {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}}, the Ichinomiya of Mimasaka Province.
== History ==
=== Foundation === According to shrine tradition, worship began at the Onokoro Iwa (碫馭盧岩) on the Yoshii River, which was regarded as a sacred rock (iwakura). The shrine itself was established during the second year of Emperor Ankan’s reign (534 CE) and subsequently moved to its current location.<ref name="origin">[http://space.geocities.jp/ninomiya_takano/contents/rekishi/yuisho/yuisho.html Takano Shrine History] (official site).</ref>
=== Development === The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a Shikinaisha of Mimasaka Province. Some theories identify the shrine in question as Takano Shrine in Ninomiya, while others propose the {{ill|Takano Shrine (Takahon-gō)|ja|高野神社 (津山市高野本郷)}} in Takahon-gō. Both remain disputed.
The shrine gained prominence in the Kamakura period, receiving patronage from notable figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, {{ill|Yamana Tokiharu|ja|山名時春}}, {{ill|Mōri Motonari|ja|毛利元就}}, and {{ill|Kobayakawa Takakage|ja|小早川隆景}}. In the Edo period, the Mori family, daimyō of the Tsuyama Domain, rebuilt the shrine. Later, the Matsudaira clan, successors to the domain, also supported the shrine.
During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was classified as a [[Ken-sha]] under the modern shrine ranking system.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Nationally Designated Important Cultural Properties === * '''Wooden Inscription Plaque''' – Designated August 2, 1901. * '''Wooden Zuishin Statues (2 figures)''' – Crafted in 1162. Designated June 28, 1956. * '''Wooden Lion Statues (2 pairs)''' – Dated to the 10th and 12th centuries. Considered among the oldest examples of this type in Japan. Designated June 8, 2004.<ref>“Newly Designated Cultural Properties,” *Monthly Cultural Properties*, Issue 489 (2004), pp. 24–25.</ref>
=== Prefectural and Municipal Designations === * '''Main Shrine''' – Prefectural designation in 1983. * '''Zuishin Gate and Roof Covering''' – Municipal designation in 1999. * '''Mukuno Tree in Unate Forest''' – Municipal designation in 1973.
== Festivals ==
* '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Summer Festival''' (July 18) * '''Autumn Festival''' (Sunday closest to October 22)
== Access == ; Address * 601 Ninomiya, Tsuyama City, Okayama Prefecture
; Transportation * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Innosho Station|ja|院庄駅}} on the {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Kishin Line|ja|姫新線}}.
== Surrounding Area == * '''Miwayama Kofuns''' – The largest keyhole-shaped Kofuns in Mimasaka. * '''Sakaku Shrine'''
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://space.geocities.jp/ninomiya_takano/top/menu.html Takano Shrine Official Website (archived)] * [http://www.city.tsuyama.lg.jp/index.cfm/22,5208,116,371,html Overview of Takano Shrine Cultural Properties (Tsuyama City Website)] * [http://www.okayama-jinjacho.or.jp/cgi-bin/jsearch.cgi?mode=detail&jcode=03005 Takano Shrine (Okayama Prefecture Shrine Association)]

'''Takano Shrine''' (高野神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tsuyama City|ja|津山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), a disputed {{ill|Ichinomiya|ja|一宮}} of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}}. Under the modern shrine ranking system, it was designated as a [[Ken-sha]] (prefectural shrine).
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated in the western part of Tsuyama City, with the sacred Mount Kami to the northwest and the Sarayama hills of Kume to the south, overlooking the Yoshii River. It is one of the three major shrines of Mimasaka Province, alongside {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}} and {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}.<ref>The other two major shrines of Mimasaka are Nakayama Shrine and Mimasaka Sōja Shrine.</ref>
Since the medieval period, the shrine enjoyed reverence from local lords, including the daimyō of the {{ill|Tsuyama Domain|ja|津山藩}}, the Mori family. The current main shrine, reconstructed by the Mori clan during the Edo period, is a designated Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture. The shrine also houses national-level Important Cultural Properties, such as statues of Zuishin (guardian deities).
== Deities ==
; Primary deity * '''Hiko Nagisa Take Uga Fuki Aezunomikoto''' (彦波限建鵜葺草葺不合尊) – The main enshrined deity.
; Associated deities * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} – Deity of {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}. * {{ill|Kagami Tsukuri no Mikoto|ja|鏡作命}} – Deity of {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}}, the Ichinomiya of Mimasaka Province.
== History ==
=== Foundation === According to shrine tradition, worship began at the Onokoro Iwa (碫馭盧岩) on the Yoshii River, which was regarded as a sacred rock (iwakura). The shrine itself was established during the second year of Emperor Ankan’s reign (534 CE) and subsequently moved to its current location.<ref name="origin">[http://space.geocities.jp/ninomiya_takano/contents/rekishi/yuisho/yuisho.html Takano Shrine History] (official site).</ref>
=== Development === The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a Shikinaisha of Mimasaka Province. Some theories identify the shrine in question as Takano Shrine in Ninomiya, while others propose the {{ill|Takano Shrine (Takahon-gō)|ja|高野神社 (津山市高野本郷)}} in Takahon-gō. Both remain disputed.
The shrine gained prominence in the Kamakura period, receiving patronage from notable figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, {{ill|Yamana Tokiharu|ja|山名時春}}, {{ill|Mōri Motonari|ja|毛利元就}}, and {{ill|Kobayakawa Takakage|ja|小早川隆景}}. In the Edo period, the Mori family, daimyō of the Tsuyama Domain, rebuilt the shrine. Later, the Matsudaira clan, successors to the domain, also supported the shrine.
During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was classified as a [[Ken-sha]] under the modern shrine ranking system.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Nationally Designated Important Cultural Properties === * '''Wooden Inscription Plaque''' – Designated August 2, 1901. * '''Wooden Zuishin Statues (2 figures)''' – Crafted in 1162. Designated June 28, 1956. * '''Wooden Lion Statues (2 pairs)''' – Dated to the 10th and 12th centuries. Considered among the oldest examples of this type in Japan. Designated June 8, 2004.<ref>“Newly Designated Cultural Properties,” *Monthly Cultural Properties*, Issue 489 (2004), pp. 24–25.</ref>
=== Prefectural and Municipal Designations === * '''Main Shrine''' – Prefectural designation in 1983. * '''Zuishin Gate and Roof Covering''' – Municipal designation in 1999. * '''Mukuno Tree in Unate Forest''' – Municipal designation in 1973.
== Festivals ==
* '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Summer Festival''' (July 18) * '''Autumn Festival''' (Sunday closest to October 22)
== Access == ; Address * 601 Ninomiya, Tsuyama City, Okayama Prefecture
; Transportation * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Innosho Station|ja|院庄駅}} on the {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Kishin Line|ja|姫新線}}.
== Surrounding Area == * '''Miwayama Kofuns''' – The largest keyhole-shaped Kofuns in Mimasaka. * '''Sakaku Shrine'''

revision.text:
'''Takano Shrine''' (高野神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tsuyama City|ja|津山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), a disputed {{ill|Ichinomiya|ja|一宮}} of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}}. Under the modern shrine ranking system, it was designated as a [[Ken-sha]] (prefectural shrine).
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated in the western part of Tsuyama City, with the sacred Mount Kami to the northwest and the Sarayama hills of Kume to the south, overlooking the Yoshii River. It is one of the three major shrines of Mimasaka Province, alongside {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}} and {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}.<ref>The other two major shrines of Mimasaka are Nakayama Shrine and Mimasaka Sōja Shrine.</ref>
Since the medieval period, the shrine enjoyed reverence from local lords, including the daimyō of the {{ill|Tsuyama Domain|ja|津山藩}}, the Mori family. The current main shrine, reconstructed by the Mori clan during the Edo period, is a designated Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture. The shrine also houses national-level Important Cultural Properties, such as statues of Zuishin (guardian deities).
== Deities ==
; Primary deity * '''Hiko Nagisa Take Uga Fuki Aezunomikoto''' (彦波限建鵜葺草葺不合尊) – The main enshrined deity.
; Associated deities * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} – Deity of {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}. * {{ill|Kagami Tsukuri no Mikoto|ja|鏡作命}} – Deity of {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}}, the Ichinomiya of Mimasaka Province.
== History ==
=== Foundation === According to shrine tradition, worship began at the Onokoro Iwa (碫馭盧岩) on the Yoshii River, which was regarded as a sacred rock (iwakura). The shrine itself was established during the second year of Emperor Ankan’s reign (534 CE) and subsequently moved to its current location.<ref name="origin">[http://space.geocities.jp/ninomiya_takano/contents/rekishi/yuisho/yuisho.html Takano Shrine History] (official site).</ref>
=== Development === The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a Shikinaisha of Mimasaka Province. Some theories identify the shrine in question as Takano Shrine in Ninomiya, while others propose the {{ill|Takano Shrine (Takahon-gō)|ja|高野神社 (津山市高野本郷)}} in Takahon-gō. Both remain disputed.
The shrine gained prominence in the Kamakura period, receiving patronage from notable figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, {{ill|Yamana Tokiharu|ja|山名時春}}, {{ill|Mōri Motonari|ja|毛利元就}}, and {{ill|Kobayakawa Takakage|ja|小早川隆景}}. In the Edo period, the Mori family, daimyō of the Tsuyama Domain, rebuilt the shrine. Later, the Matsudaira clan, successors to the domain, also supported the shrine.
During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was classified as a [[Ken-sha]] under the modern shrine ranking system.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Nationally Designated Important Cultural Properties === * '''Wooden Inscription Plaque''' – Designated August 2, 1901. * '''Wooden Zuishin Statues (2 figures)''' – Crafted in 1162. Designated June 28, 1956. * '''Wooden Lion Statues (2 pairs)''' – Dated to the 10th and 12th centuries. Considered among the oldest examples of this type in Japan. Designated June 8, 2004.<ref>“Newly Designated Cultural Properties,” *Monthly Cultural Properties*, Issue 489 (2004), pp. 24–25.</ref>
=== Prefectural and Municipal Designations === * '''Main Shrine''' – Prefectural designation in 1983. * '''Zuishin Gate and Roof Covering''' – Municipal designation in 1999. * '''Mukuno Tree in Unate Forest''' – Municipal designation in 1973.
== Festivals ==
* '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Summer Festival''' (July 18) * '''Autumn Festival''' (Sunday closest to October 22)
== Access == ; Address * 601 Ninomiya, Tsuyama City, Okayama Prefecture
; Transportation * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Innosho Station|ja|院庄駅}} on the {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Kishin Line|ja|姫新線}}.
== Surrounding Area == * '''Miwayama Kofuns''' – The largest keyhole-shaped Kofuns in Mimasaka. * '''Sakaku Shrine'''
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://space.geocities.jp/ninomiya_takano/top/menu.html Takano Shrine Official Website (archived)] * [http://www.city.tsuyama.lg.jp/index.cfm/22,5208,116,371,html Overview of Takano Shrine Cultural Properties (Tsuyama City Website)] * [http://www.okayama-jinjacho.or.jp/cgi-bin/jsearch.cgi?mode=detail&jcode=03005 Takano Shrine (Okayama Prefecture Shrine Association)]

'''Takano Shrine''' (高野神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tsuyama City|ja|津山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), a disputed {{ill|Ichinomiya|ja|一宮}} of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}}. Under the modern shrine ranking system, it was designated as a [[Ken-sha]] (prefectural shrine).
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated in the western part of Tsuyama City, with the sacred Mount Kami to the northwest and the Sarayama hills of Kume to the south, overlooking the Yoshii River. It is one of the three major shrines of Mimasaka Province, alongside {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}} and {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}.<ref>The other two major shrines of Mimasaka are Nakayama Shrine and Mimasaka Sōja Shrine.</ref>
Since the medieval period, the shrine enjoyed reverence from local lords, including the daimyō of the {{ill|Tsuyama Domain|ja|津山藩}}, the Mori family. The current main shrine, reconstructed by the Mori clan during the Edo period, is a designated Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture. The shrine also houses national-level Important Cultural Properties, such as statues of Zuishin (guardian deities).
== Deities ==
; Primary deity * '''Hiko Nagisa Take Uga Fuki Aezunomikoto''' (彦波限建鵜葺草葺不合尊) – The main enshrined deity.
; Associated deities * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} – Deity of {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}. * {{ill|Kagami Tsukuri no Mikoto|ja|鏡作命}} – Deity of {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}}, the Ichinomiya of Mimasaka Province.
== History ==
=== Foundation === According to shrine tradition, worship began at the Onokoro Iwa (碫馭盧岩) on the Yoshii River, which was regarded as a sacred rock (iwakura). The shrine itself was established during the second year of Emperor Ankan’s reign (534 CE) and subsequently moved to its current location.<ref name="origin">[http://space.geocities.jp/ninomiya_takano/contents/rekishi/yuisho/yuisho.html Takano Shrine History] (official site).</ref>
=== Development === The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a Shikinaisha of Mimasaka Province. Some theories identify the shrine in question as Takano Shrine in Ninomiya, while others propose the {{ill|Takano Shrine (Takahon-gō)|ja|高野神社 (津山市高野本郷)}} in Takahon-gō. Both remain disputed.
The shrine gained prominence in the Kamakura period, receiving patronage from notable figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, {{ill|Yamana Tokiharu|ja|山名時春}}, {{ill|Mōri Motonari|ja|毛利元就}}, and {{ill|Kobayakawa Takakage|ja|小早川隆景}}. In the Edo period, the Mori family, daimyō of the Tsuyama Domain, rebuilt the shrine. Later, the Matsudaira clan, successors to the domain, also supported the shrine.
During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was classified as a [[Ken-sha]] under the modern shrine ranking system.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Nationally Designated Important Cultural Properties === * '''Wooden Inscription Plaque''' – Designated August 2, 1901. * '''Wooden Zuishin Statues (2 figures)''' – Crafted in 1162. Designated June 28, 1956. * '''Wooden Lion Statues (2 pairs)''' – Dated to the 10th and 12th centuries. Considered among the oldest examples of this type in Japan. Designated June 8, 2004.<ref>“Newly Designated Cultural Properties,” *Monthly Cultural Properties*, Issue 489 (2004), pp. 24–25.</ref>
=== Prefectural and Municipal Designations === * '''Main Shrine''' – Prefectural designation in 1983. * '''Zuishin Gate and Roof Covering''' – Municipal designation in 1999. * '''Mukuno Tree in Unate Forest''' – Municipal designation in 1973.
== Festivals ==
* '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Summer Festival''' (July 18) * '''Autumn Festival''' (Sunday closest to October 22)
== Access == ; Address * 601 Ninomiya, Tsuyama City, Okayama Prefecture
; Transportation * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Innosho Station|ja|院庄駅}} on the {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Kishin Line|ja|姫新線}}.
== Surrounding Area == * '''Miwayama Kofuns''' – The largest keyhole-shaped Kofuns in Mimasaka. * '''Sakaku Shrine'''

revision.comment: Bot: Reformatted page - moved categories, interwikis, DEFAULTSORT, and removed duplicates.
revision.text:
'''Takano Shrine''' (高野神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tsuyama City|ja|津山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor), a disputed {{ill|Ichinomiya|ja|一宮}} of {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}}. Under the modern shrine ranking system, it was designated as a [[Ken-sha]] (prefectural shrine).
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated in the western part of Tsuyama City, with the sacred Mount Kami to the northwest and the Sarayama hills of Kume to the south, overlooking the Yoshii River. It is one of the three major shrines of Mimasaka Province, alongside {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}} and {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}.<ref>The other two major shrines of Mimasaka are Nakayama Shrine and Mimasaka Sōja Shrine.</ref>
Since the medieval period, the shrine enjoyed reverence from local lords, including the daimyō of the {{ill|Tsuyama Domain|ja|津山藩}}, the Mori family. The current main shrine, reconstructed by the Mori clan during the Edo period, is a designated Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture. The shrine also houses national-level Important Cultural Properties, such as statues of Zuishin (guardian deities).
== Deities ==
; Primary deity * '''Hiko Nagisa Take Uga Fuki Aezunomikoto''' (彦波限建鵜葺草葺不合尊) – The main enshrined deity.
; Associated deities * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} – Deity of {{ill|Mimasaka Sōjagū|ja|美作総社宮}}. * {{ill|Kagami Tsukuri no Mikoto|ja|鏡作命}} – Deity of {{ill|Nakayama Shrine|ja|中山神社}}, the Ichinomiya of Mimasaka Province.
== History ==
=== Foundation === According to shrine tradition, worship began at the Onokoro Iwa (碫馭盧岩) on the Yoshii River, which was regarded as a sacred rock (iwakura). The shrine itself was established during the second year of Emperor Ankan’s reign (534 CE) and subsequently moved to its current location.<ref name="origin">[http://space.geocities.jp/ninomiya_takano/contents/rekishi/yuisho/yuisho.html Takano Shrine History] (official site).</ref>
=== Development === The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as a Shikinaisha of Mimasaka Province. Some theories identify the shrine in question as Takano Shrine in Ninomiya, while others propose the {{ill|Takano Shrine (Takahon-gō)|ja|高野神社 (津山市高野本郷)}} in Takahon-gō. Both remain disputed.
The shrine gained prominence in the Kamakura period, receiving patronage from notable figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, {{ill|Yamana Tokiharu|ja|山名時春}}, {{ill|Mōri Motonari|ja|毛利元就}}, and {{ill|Kobayakawa Takakage|ja|小早川隆景}}. In the Edo period, the Mori family, daimyō of the Tsuyama Domain, rebuilt the shrine. Later, the Matsudaira clan, successors to the domain, also supported the shrine.
During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was classified as a [[Ken-sha]] under the modern shrine ranking system.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Nationally Designated Important Cultural Properties === * '''Wooden Inscription Plaque''' – Designated August 2, 1901. * '''Wooden Zuishin Statues (2 figures)''' – Crafted in 1162. Designated June 28, 1956. * '''Wooden Lion Statues (2 pairs)''' – Dated to the 10th and 12th centuries. Considered among the oldest examples of this type in Japan. Designated June 8, 2004.<ref>“Newly Designated Cultural Properties,” *Monthly Cultural Properties*, Issue 489 (2004), pp. 24–25.</ref>
=== Prefectural and Municipal Designations === * '''Main Shrine''' – Prefectural designation in 1983. * '''Zuishin Gate and Roof Covering''' – Municipal designation in 1999. * '''Mukuno Tree in Unate Forest''' – Municipal designation in 1973.
== Festivals ==
* '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Summer Festival''' (July 18) * '''Autumn Festival''' (Sunday closest to October 22)
== Access == ; Address * 601 Ninomiya, Tsuyama City, Okayama Prefecture
; Transportation * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Innosho Station|ja|院庄駅}} on the {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Kishin Line|ja|姫新線}}.
== Surrounding Area == * '''Miwayama Kofuns''' – The largest keyhole-shaped Kofuns in Mimasaka. * '''Sakaku Shrine'''
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://space.geocities.jp/ninomiya_takano/top/menu.html Takano Shrine Official Website (archived)] * [http://www.city.tsuyama.lg.jp/index.cfm/22,5208,116,371,html Overview of Takano Shrine Cultural Properties (Tsuyama City Website)] * [http://www.okayama-jinjacho.or.jp/cgi-bin/jsearch.cgi?mode=detail&jcode=03005 Takano Shrine (Okayama Prefecture Shrine Association)]
